How to Choose,
Implement and Wow Others With
Your Best Ideas
After you’ve come up with ideas — especially if you’ve finished and applied the
techniques in my Idea Power course, you may find you have a problem…
More ideas than you know what to do with.
How do you tell which are your best ones?
And most importantly: How do you put them to work for you?
Not to worry, a simple 4-step process will help you easily discover the best ones, and
re-purpose the rest. And I will lay that process out for you in this report. And be sure to
stick around for the second half, where I give you the 10 best ways I’ve found to get
others to “buy into” those ideas.
My 4-Step Process for Choosing Your Best Ideas
1. Bank Your Ideas — Obviously, your ideas won’t do you (or anyone else) any
good at all if you can’t find them after you come up with them. So numero uno is
to have a centralized (and organized) place to put all your ideas.
You can use a notebook, a Word doc, a spreadsheet, notecards… whatever
works best for you.
TIP: Putting them in a spreadsheet makes it easy to sort by subject, or any
rating system you might use (see below).
2. Rank Your Ideas — Take a step back, wait a day or two. When you go back to
it, maybe even pretend someone else came up with the ideas. Evaluate them as
if you’re someone whose opinion you respect. Or who will somehow be impacted
by the implementation of what you come up with. Divide your ideas into three
groups: 1) Worth considering, 2) Maybe worth considering and 3) Not worth
considering.
TIP: Don’t throw away those “not worth considering” ideas. They may be
well worth another look in the future. Or the kindling that sparks more,
better ideas.
3. Agree on Three — Pick the three ideas with the most potential from your
“Worth considering” list… the three your gut says might work. Don’t overthink it!
TIP: Get help. Discuss your top three ideas with anyone who will listen.
This is definitely a case where two (or more) heads are a LOT better than
one. They may send you back to the drawing board on one or more of
your ideas. Or give you some even BETTER ideas.
PRO TIP: Assemble — online or off — a regular idea group with some
friends or associates to brainstorm and evaluate ideas together.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 3 --

4. Don’t Stew… Do! — Ideas love speed. It gets them moving, gives them
momentum, enables you to get feedback.
So take your best idea and implement it as soon as you can, however you can.
In Silicon Valley, there’s something they call an MVP — a minimum viable
product, which is a quick and dirty working or even non-working mock-up of the
product.
This concept works for ideas, too.
Put it into practice in some small way. Write it up. Draw it out. Take it for a spin.
Collect feedback.
You may find your idea falls flat.
Not a problem since you haven’t invested much time. In that case, simply move
on to idea number two.
TIP: If you can’t find a way to put your whole idea into action, try starting
with just a part of it. That way you can still kick things off and observe the
results.
Now, once you’ve chosen your best ideas, it’s time to get others on board. Here are 10
of my favorite ways I’ve used to sell your best ideas.
My Top 10 Best Ways to Get Buy-In for Your Ideas
Once you’ve selected your best ideas, the next step is often to get others onboard.
Perhaps you need approval from a boss, client, business partner… or maybe your
spouse.
And even if approval isn’t required, it always helps to get “buy in” from anyone who will
help implement your idea, benefit from it or be impacted by it.
So here are ten ways to become a more effective seller of ideas…
1. Solve a Problem — Be clear on the problem your idea will solve, the
opportunity it will help take advantage of, and all the benefits to be reaped.
Remember, people don’t need drills — they need holes. (And sometimes they
just need to stir paint.)
2. Agitate the Pain — People care about solving problems when they’re
feeling the pain of them. So you may have to remind them of the pain — even
agitate it a bit. Try to reflect on all the current and future problems and potential
pain in the current situation, and describe it with vivid detail before presenting
your solution.
3. Tell Stories — People love stories. So use them as a tool. Maybe start with
one that dramatizes the problem your idea solves… the solution… how you came
up with the idea… or how this idea (or another like it) has been used successfully
elsewhere.
4. Paint Pictures — Use descriptive words to help people envision what life
will be like after the idea is implemented. And how awful it will be if the idea is not
implemented. Paint pictures of the idea in action. Don’t just say, “this idea will
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 3 --

lead to an increase in sales.” Instead, say: “imagine thousands of customers
flocking to our stores, tearing the product from the shelves.”
5. Sell to the Right Person — Selling to the wrong person is one of the
biggest reasons ideas flop. Is the person you’re about to sell to likely to say
“yes”? Do they have the authority to give the go-ahead? Does it make more
sense to sell to someone else?
6. Don’t Forget the Benefits — Don’t just talk about why your idea is so great.
Figure out and clearly enumerate why your idea is the best option for the person
you’re trying to convince. How will it improve their life? Will it make them look
better, feel better? Will it help them make more money, get them a promotion?
7. Give Away Equity — Give the person (or people) you’re selling to a feeling
of ownership in the idea — a stake in its success. You’re getting their input, right
— either on how to refine your idea or for additional ideas to go along with it?
Show them how their feedback will help you shape the idea. (And then be sure to
actually MAKE some changes based on that feedback.)
8. Make It Easy — Make clear how simple your idea will be to implement —
how little effort, time and/or money it will take. Show that the benefits easily
outweigh the costs.
9. Demonstrate — Do whatever you can to bring your idea to life. Write it up.
Act it out. Create a model. Describe it with colorful, lifelike details. People won’t
buy into what they can’t easily picture. (Note to copywriters: The more you flesh
out your idea, the easier it will be to sell. And ironically, fleshing it out will help
you refine it.)
10. Make It Urgent — It’s not that hard to convince someone that an idea is
good. Or that it should be implemented. It’s quite another to convince them that it
should be implemented immediately and not just put on a shelf where it will likely
be forgotten. So remember to sell the urgency and emphasize the high price of
procrastination.
In business, for example, stories of competitors bearing down or a window of
opportunity closing convey a need for urgent action.
Remember: Ideas have no inherent value on their own.
They are only valuable when harnessed to action.
So don’t let your ideas gather dust — start putting them to work NOW.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 3 --
